title-group.article-title: Isolation, Identification, and Antibiogram Characteristics of Staphylococcus aureus From Dairy Cows Kept Under Extensive Management System, in the Uba DebreTsehay District of Southern Ethiopia

aff.named-content: School of Veterinary Medicine, Veterinary Microbiology, Immunology and Public Health

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Author(s). Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Bovine mastitis is a widespread and important disease of cows that affects the global production and quality of milk in the dairy industry. A cross‐sectional study conducted from February to August 2022 in the Uba Debretsehay District aimed to determine the prevalence of bovine mastitis, assess the associated risk factors and the antibiotic susceptibility profiles of Staphylococcus aureus isolates from mastitis‐positive cows. A total of 432 cows were screened through clinical examinations and the California mastitis test to determine the occurrence of clinical and sub‐clinical mastitis respectively. A milk sample was collected from mastitis‐positive cows and standard microbiological procedures were used to identify S. aureus. Antimicrobial susceptibility testing was performed using 10 antimicrobials following the Kirby–Bauer disc diffusion method. The overall prevalence of mastitis was 44.2% (191/432; 95% CI = 0.37–0.51) and 41% (702/1728) at the cow and quarter level respectively. The odds for the occurrence of mastitis significantly decreased without tick infestation and or injury (OR = 0.15; 95% CI = 0.07–0.28), and the previous history of mastitis (OR = 0.28; 95% CI = 0.17–0.47). Cows in mid‐stage lactation were 2.13 times more likely to develop mastitis than early‐stage lactating cows. Additionally, cows kept with in larger herds were 1.57 times more prone to mastitis than those on small herd size. Out of the 191 mastitis‐positive milk samples, S. aureus was isolated in 2.6% (5/191) of these samples using the BioLog microbial identification system. S. aureus isolates showed no susceptibility (0%) to penicillin G or tetracycline, but exhibited 100% susceptibility to amoxicillin‐clavulanate, cefoxitin, ciprofloxacin, chloramphenicol, oxacillin, trimethoprim‐sulphamethoxazole, and gentamicin. Mastitis is a severe problem for dairy production in the study area, and the resistance of S. aureus to commonly used antibiotics was observed. Therefore, proactive management strategies including adequate sanitation of the dairy environment, regular screening of animals, and rational use of antibiotic agents were recommended to maintain the productivity and well‐being of dairy cows in the study areas.

Bovine mastitis (44.2%) is prevalent in the study area, with significant resistance observed in S. auerus isolates for regularly used drugs. Improved proactive management strategies, including better sanitation, regular screening, and rational antibiotic use, are recommended.

fn.p: Funding: The authors received no specific funding for this work.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Bovine mastitis is a quite common and important disease of cows that affects the production and quality of milk in the dairy industry worldwide (Halasa et al. 2007, Song et al. 2020). It can appear clinically and sub‐clinically (Ruegg 2017, Mbindyo et al. 2020). Clinical mastitis is characterized by having systemic symptoms in the cow and visible abnormalities in the udder and milk including flakes and clots (Mbindyo et al. 2020, Jamali et al. 2018). On the contrary, subclinical mastitis is usually accompanied by a decline in milk production without observable clinical signs or abnormalities in the udder or milk (Mbindyo et al. 2020, Ndahetuye et al. 2020). Due to this feature, subclinical mastitis is not recognized easily to diagnose and persists longer in the herd and is also related to higher financial losses compared with clinical mastitis (Mbindyo et al. 2020, Abrahmsén et al. 2014). A lot of economic losses associated with bovine mastitis are due to a decrease in milk production, poor milk quality, extra labour, expensive treatment costs, culling, and death (Jamali et al. 2018, Bradley 2002).

Infectious and non‐infectious factors cause mastitis. Injury, cold, bruising, or improper or rough milking is the causes of non‐infectious mastitis. Bacterial, mycotic, or algal pathogens are the causes of infectious mastitis. However, an infection caused by bacteria or mycotic pathogens is the primary cause of mastitis (Seyoum et al. 2018). Farmers often rely on antibiotics to manage mastitis. However, excessive use of antibiotics in livestock can lead to the development of antimicrobial‐resistant strains, financial losses, and a decrease in the effectiveness of mastitis prevention and management (Khasapane et al. 2024).

The occurrence of antibiotic‐resistant bacteria in cases of bovine mastitis and the potential for these bacteria to be transmitted to humans through the consumption of unpasteurized dairy products are two major public health issues (Jamali et al. 2018, Bradley 2002, Khasapane et al. 2024).

S. aureus is a significant pathogen that causes mastitis and poses a concern to food safety as well as antibiotic resistance, among other etiological agents [9]. According to (Abebe et al. 2016), it is a versatile pathogen that affects both humans and animals and can cause a wide range of diseases. It is also a major cause of both clinical and subclinical mastitis in dairy cows, which results in significant economic losses globally. S. aureus is also reported as the most common cause of mastitis in dairy cows from Ethiopia (Seyoum et al. 2018, Getahun et al. 2008).

Although it is not well established in rural areas, several studies were conducted regarding the prevalence of bovine mastitis and S. aureus involved mastitis in urban and peri‐urban areas in Ethiopia these days (Seyoum et al. 2018, Abebe et al. 2016, Getahun et al. 2008, Abera et al. 2013). Additionally, there is also lack of proper identification of the causative agent of mastitis which led to the indiscriminate use of antibiotics, consequently increasing drug resistance development and the rapid emergence of multidrug resistance strains (Sharma et al. 2011). The studies on the prevalence of bovine mastitis and knowledge concerning pathogens involved in mastitis remain still very limited in marginalized rural areas of the country (Kumbe et al. 2020, Wubishet et al. 2013). The finding from this study is crucial to fill knowledge gap by providing region‐specific prevalence data, which is essential for designing targeted interventions. Therefore, the present study was aimed to determine the prevalence of bovine mastitis, associated risk factors, and antibiotic susceptibility pattern of S. aureus in mastitis‐positive zebu cow milk in extensive production system in Uba Debretsehaye district, South Ethiopia.

sec.title: Study Area
sec.p: The study was conducted in Uba Debretsehay district (Figure 1), Gofa Zone, South Ethiopia. Uba Debretsehay district is located about 549 km south–west of capital Addis Ababa at 6004′10′′N latitude and 36052′44′′E longitude with an altitude range of 1100–2100m from above sea level. The size of livestock is an important indicator of a household's wealth position. This sub‐sector of the economy has been seriously affected by the continuous shrinkage or decline of grazing land and the high prevalence of diseases, particularly in the low‐land pastoral villages of the study area. The total livestock population in Uba Debretsehay district is estimated as cattle: 269,906, sheep and goats: 183,703, equine: 31,488 and poultry: 265,252. The district has a herd size between 16 to 30 cattle per each household (UDAO 2022).

caption.p: Map of the study area created using ArcGIS v10.3 software.

sec.title: Study Animals
sec.p: A total of 432 lactating cows from 15 herds kept under extensive management system were included in the study. None lactating cows and other cattle categories were excluded from the study. The herds studied had an average herd size of 110 (median = 70), including lactating cows, dry cows, bulls, heifers, and weaned calves while the median number of lactating cows was 32. The studied cows had an average milk yield of approximately 1.5 L per cow per day. Lactating cows are milked twice a day, in the morning before grazing time and in the evening after grazing. Depending on the farmer's preference and the size of the teats, milking methods may include stripping (milking with the thumb and index finger), squeezing (milking with five fingers), or both. Generally, the lactating cows were not milked to potential rather focusing on cows and calves’ live‐saving as much as possible. The herd is totally unimproved local Zebu cows and milk production is of subsistent type. All (100%) of the herds do not wash the udder. The breeding practice was done only by bull mating.

sec.title: Study Design and Sample Size Determination
sec.p: A cross‐sectional study was undertaken from February to August 2022 in pastoral areas of Uba Debretsehay District, Southern Ethiopia.

Study areas were chosen purposefully based on herds managed under pastoralist conditions and livestock population using data from district agricultural offices. Areas covered were Shelle‐Bune, Garo‐Qorpha, Hoshele Shambara, Beto, Bala, Kencho, and Zaba. Herds were purposively selected based on the presence of lactating cows, and husbandry practices. Simple random sampling was used for each lactating cow.

The sample size was established using the sample size determination method outlined by Thrusfield (Thrusfield 2005), with a 95% confidence interval and 5% desired precision. Since there were no previous studies conducted in the area, an expected prevalence of 50% was assumed. This corresponded to a required minimum sample size of 384 lactating cows by the formula given as follows. n=1.962Pexp1−Pexpd2
Where: n = number of sample size
P exp = Expected prevalence = 50%
d 2 = Absolute precision = 5%
CI = Confidence interval (95%)
Based on this formula, the sample size was supposed to be 384 lactating cows. However, 432 samples were taken deliberately to maximize the precision of the study.

sec.title: Data Collection
sec.p: The data record format was prepared and used to collect data about the potential risk factors in the study area. The risk factors considered were age, parity, stage of lactation, presence/absence of tick and/or lesion on the udder and/or teat, housing, and floor type. The age of the study animals was determined from dentition/ history and categorized as young (≤4 years), adults (4–6 years) and old (≥7 years). In the present study, herds with ≤ 15, 15 to 30, and >30 milking cows were classified into small, medium and large‐size, respectively. Similarly, the stage of lactation was categorized into three levels namely 1–3 months post‐partum (early lactation), 3–6 months (middle lactation), and months greater than or equal to 6 (late lactation). Parity was also categorized into primiparous having produced one calf and multiparous when more than one calves (Emon et al. 2024).

sec.title: Milk Sampling and Transportation
sec.p: Milk samples were collected after cleaning the teat ends with 70% alcohol, and an aseptic milk sample was collected in a 10 mL sterile sampling tube. The samples collected in a single day were then transported on the same day in an ice‐cooled box to Wolaita Sodo University Veterinary Microbiology laboratory for isolation of S. aureus. The samples were immediately cultured or stored at 4 °C for a maximum of 24 h, until cultured on standard bacteriological media.

sec.title: Detection of Mastitis
sec.p: Clinical mastitis‐positive cows were defined after careful udder inspection and thorough palpation to detect any possible fibrosis, swellings that are inflammatory, obvious wounds, infestation by ticks, tissue atrophy, and enlargement of the lymph nodes above the udder. The size and consistency of the udder quarter were examined for any abnormalities, such as asymmetrical symmetry, swelling, stiffness, and blindness. Each udder quarter's milk secretion was analysed for viscosity and appearance to check for the presence of clots, flakes, blood, and watery discharges (Seyoum et al. 2018, Abebe et al. 2016, Dabele et al. 2021, Tezera and Aman Ali 2021).

A cow was considered to be affected by subclinical mastitis when they had no visible signs in the udder or in the milk, and further the collected milk sample screened by the CMT according to Quinn et al. (Quinn et al. 2002). Briefly, a squirt of milk from each quarter of the udder was placed in each of the cups on the CMT paddle, and an equal amount of 3% CMT reagent was added to each cup and mixed well (Seyoum et al. 2018). Then, the result was indirectly evaluated by CMT using the Scandinavian scoring system grade 1–5, where 1 indicates a negative result (no gel formation, no indicative colour change), 2 is traceable (possible inflammation), and 3 or above indicates a positive result where 5 has the most gel formation and deep blue/violet colour change (Ndahetuye et al. 2020). A cow was defined as positive for mastitis either if she had at least one positive quarter (CMT ≥3) as sub‐clinical mastitis or with signs of illness and/or visible inflammatory signs of the udder, and with visible abnormality in milk as clinical mastitis[6]. A cow that was found positive for mastitis based on either clinical examination or CMT was sampled for culturing and identification of S. aureus.

sec.title: Isolation and Identification of S. aureus
sec.p: A loop full of milk sample collected from mastitis‐positive cow was cultured on blood agar base enriched with 7% sheep blood. The inoculated plates were then incubated aerobically at 37 °C for 24–48 h. Under a light microscope, the sizes, forms, and cell configurations of each of those suspected S. aureus colonies were scrutinized after being Gram‐stained. Gram‐stained smears from characteristic colonies were thought to be S. aureus because they contained Gram‐positive cocci in clumped, irregular clusters that looked like grapes (Seyoum et al. 2018). To obtain a pure culture, the suspected S. aureus colonies were subsequently subcultured on nutrient agar plates and incubated at 37 °C for 24–48 h (Quinn et al. 2002). Suspected colonies of S. aureus were then selected and further sub cultured on mannitol salt agars and incubated aerobically at 37 °C for 24 h. S. aureus colonies which produced a yellow pigment on the medium were subjected to slide catalase and coagulase[9, 23] and oxidation tests (Seyoum et al. 2018, Quinn et al. 2002, Bihon et al. 2019). Samples were considered positive for S. aureus when confirmed by the OmniLog/Biolog (fully automated coated microplate based bacterial identification system). In brief, a single colony from the 24‐h BHI agar culture was carefully picked using wooden applicator sticks and spread as a thin film directly onto a designated spot on a MALDI target plate. This thin layer was then covered with 70% formic acid and left to dry at room temperature. Afterward, the sample was coated with a matrix, specifically a‐cyano‐4‐hydroxycinnamic acid (HCCA portion), and allowed to dry once more at room temperature. E. coli (ATCC 8739) was utilized as a quality control measure for the machine's reading. The plate was subsequently inserted into the MALDI‐ToF machine, which was operated, and the results were displayed on the connected computer.

sec.title: Antimicrobial Susceptibility Test
sec.p: Antimicrobial resistance tests were performed by standard disc diffusion technique (Quinn et al. 2011) using ten different antibiotics belonging to 8 pharmacological groups including penicillin G (10 µg), gentamicin (10 µg), tetracycline (30 µg), amoxicillin + clavulanic acid (30 µg), oxacillin (1 µg), erythromycin (15 µg), cefoxitin (30 µg), chloramphenicol (30 µg), sulphadimethoxazole‐trimethoprim (25 µg) and ciprofloxacin (10 µg) (Oxoid Ltd., Hampshire, UK). Briefly, each isolated bacterial colony from pure fresh culture was put into a test tube containing 5 mL tryptone soya broth and incubated at 37 °C for 6 h. More isolated colonies were either introduced or sterile saline solution was used to modify the culture broth's turbidity until it was typically comparable to that of 0.5 McFarland standards. The preparation of Mueller‐Hinton agar plates (Becton, Dickinson and Company, New Jersey, USA) followed the manufacturer's instructions. A sterile cotton swab was dipped into the suspension and rotated against the side of the tube to drain off any surplus fluid and then swabbed uniformly on the surface of Mueller‐Hinton agar plates. Then, antibiotic disks were added using sterile forceps after the plates had dried. The antibiotic disks were gently pressed onto the agar to ensure firm contact with the agar surface and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Following this, the diameter of the inhibition zone formed around each disk was measured using a black surface, reflected light, and a transparent ruler laying over the plates. The measurements were classified as sensitive, intermediate, and resistant according to the American CLSI standards for AST result comparison (CLSI 2023).

sec.title: Data Management and Statistical Analysis
sec.p: All the data recorded during the course of the study including potential risk factors were coded and entered into the Microsoft Excel database system and subjected to descriptive statistics. The prevalence was calculated by dividing the number of mastitis‐positive animals by the total number of animals examined times of 100% (Thrusfield 2005).

SPSS program version 20 analysis software was used to analyse the data. The association between the dependent variables (the prevalence of mastitis) and the risk factors was determined using univariable and multivariable logistic regressions at 95% confidence intervals. We considered an association between variables statistically significant if the p‐value was below 0.05.

sec.title: Prevalence of Bovine Mastitis
sec.p: From 432 lactating cows examined, 191 (44.2%; 95% CI = 0.37–0.51) were found to be positive for mastitis based on clinical examination and CMT (Table 1). Out of 1728 quarters examined from all sampled cows, 702 (41%) were found to be positive for mastitis as shown below in Table 1 and 3.3% had blind teat.

caption.p: Prevalence of mastitis at the cow/quarter level and forms of mastitis.

sec.title: Association of Risk Factors With Prevalence of Mastitis
sec.p: According to the current results, tick infestation and or injury, and the previous history of mastitis were found significant factors for causing mastitis (p < 0.05). However, the other predetermined listed factors have got no significant association (Table 2).

caption.p: Final multivariable logistic regression model describing the associations between risk factors and mastitis in dairy cows in pastoral areas of Uba Debretsehay District, South Ethiopia.

sec.title: Isolation and Identification of S. aureus
sec.p: Out of 191 milk samples (including clinical and subclinical cases) that were tested for S. aureus, 110 showed bacterial growth. However, since the objective of this study was to isolate S. aureus, further biochemical bacteriological tests were conducted by taking only those colonies presumptive of this species. Finally, S. aureus was isolated at a rate of 2.6% (5/191; 95% CI = 0.045–0.097) based on the fully automated OminiLog test.

sec.title: Antimicrobial Susceptibility of S. aureus
sec.p: The antibiotics tested for resistance patterns in this study were those which were proved to be often available and routinely used in the study areas for the treatment of animal and human ailments. All S. aureus isolates were subjected to an antimicrobial susceptibility test of ten different antibiotics as indicated below in Table 3.

caption.p: Summary of the result of the antimicrobial susceptibility test.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In rural Ethiopia, where dairy farming is a key livelihood, such a high prevalence (44.2%) suggests substantial economic losses due to reduced milk yield, quality, and increased veterinary costs. The findings was relatively consistent with the report of Abera et al. (Abera et al. 2013) in Adama town, Girma et al. (Grima et al. 2021) around Holeta areas, and Sarba and Tola (Sarba and Tola 2016) in Ambo district of West Shoa Zone, which showed prevalence of 46.7%, 44.1%, and 41.7% respectively. However, the present finding was lower than the findings of 62.6% in dairy herds at Hawassa milk shed of South Ethiopia (Abebe et al. 2016), 64.3% in selected districts of the Eastern Harrarghe Zone (Zeryehun and Abera 2017), 74.3% in the Addis Ababa area (Zeryehun et al. 2013), 75.22% in Jimma Town (Sori et al. 2011), 71% around Holeta Town (Mekibib et al. 2010), 56.5% in Batu and its surrounding (Bedacha and Menghistu 2011), and 56.16% in West Algeria (Benhamed et al. 2011). Besides, the finding of the present study was higher than Dabele et al. (Dabele et al. 2021) and Belay et al. (Belay et al. 2022) who reported 30.5% and 17.1% in selected districts in the West Shoa Zone, and Gamo Zone of Ethiopia respectively. The disparities in prevalence report in the present and other similar studies could be attributed to different management systems, agro climatic conditions, environmental hygiene, epidemiological, and breed‐related factors (Radostitis et al. 2007, Ondiek and Kemboi 2018). Under Ethiopian condition including the study locale most dairy farmers do not exercise management practice such as teat dipping, good milking procedure, dry cow therapy and lack diagnostic facilities which could significantly impact the health and productivity of dairy cows.

The overall prevalence of mastitis at the quarter level, 41%, found in this study was much lower than the findings of Abebe et al. (Abebe et al. 2016) and higher than that of Dabele et al. (Dabele et al. 2021), who reported 74.7% in Hawassa and 8.3% in selected districts of West Shoa Zone respectively. Upon comparison of the relative infection rates of the four quarters, the hind quarters had a slightly higher infection rate than the front quarters. This finding is in accord with the findings of Zeryehun et al. (Zeryehun et al. 2013) in smallholder dairy farms in Addis Ababa. This would be associated with the high production capacity of hindquarters which are reported to yield 60% of production (Radostitis et al. 2007) and the high chance of getting faecal and environmental contamination (Sori et al. 2011). The prevalence of blind mammary quarters (3.3%) closely agrees with the result of the Sarba and Tola (Sarba and Tola 2016) report in the Ambo district. However, this finding was higher than the report of Deng et al. (Deng et al. 2015) who recorded 0.2% in Gambella. The large number of blind mammary quarters observed in this study may be a result of both the absence of culling, which would have removed a potential source of mammary infections for the cows, and the substantial impact of mastitis in the study area.

Moreover, the mammary quarter may become blind due to inadequate screening for subclinical mastitis and a tardy or non‐existent treatment of clinical instances. It can be postulated that high subclinical mastitis and decreased milk output are associated with blind mammary quarters, which may have an effect on food security (Sarba and Tola 2016). Furthermore, mammary quarter blindness may arise from missing subclinical mastitis diagnoses and from treating clinical instances slowly or not at all. It is likely that blind mammary glands and high subclinical mastitis levels have an impact on food security through reduced milk supply.

The overall prevalence of bovine mastitis in this study was not found to be statistically significant among the age groups; however, higher prevalence of mastitis recorded in older cows than young and adult cows. This finding is consistent with that of Sarba and Tola (Sarba and Tola 2016), and Fufa et al. (Fufa et al. 2013) who reported an increased prevalence of mastitis with advancing age of the cows. This is because older cows have the largest teats and more relaxed sphincter muscles, which makes them liable to the accessibility of infectious agents (Radostitis et al. 2007) and it also could be due to an increased probability of getting mastitis as the age increases.

Although parity had no statistically significant effect on the prevalence of mastitis in the current study, cows with many calves were at greater risk (50%) than those of cows having produced one calve (38%). A similar finding was reported by Haftu et al. (Haftu et al. 2012), and Mekibib et al. (Mekibib et al. 2010). This might be due to the increased opportunity of teat patency and degree and frequency of previous exposure in old cow (Radostitis et al. 2007). Moreover, Jha et al. (Jha et al. 2010) also explained the contribution of better active mononuclear leukocyte function in primiparous cows than in multiparous cows.

The lactation stage was not significantly associated with the occurrence of mastitis in lactating cows. Early‐stage lactating cows (57.3%) were more likely to develop mastitis than middle and late‐stage lactating cows that concurs with previous reports (Tezera and Aman Ali 2021). However, our finding disagrees with Dabele et al. (Dabele et al. 2021) and Feyissa et al. (Feyissa et al. 2023) who reported that the animals with early lactation stage have less probability to be affected by mastitis than those in the above middle lactation stage. This could be due to the delayed diapedesis of neutrophils to the mammary gland in recently calved cow and at late lactation, there is a decrement of neutrophil concentration when the cows reach to dry off (Workineh et al. 2002) and increased oxidative stress and reduced antioxidant defence mechanisms during early lactation (Sharma et al. 2011). Moreover, the absence of a dry cow therapy regime could be among the major factors contributing to higher prevalence at early lactation (Tezera and Aman Ali 2021, Green et al. 2008), the high rates of new infection following drying off may be associated with the lack of flushing action of milking (Sarba and Tola 2016).
It was observed that all the herds with more than thirty lactating cows were had the highest prevalence of mastitis than milking cows with a herd size of less than or equal to fifteen.

Contagious mastitis will spread even more if the practice of cleaning the udder before milking is abandoned. The higher prevalence of mastitis occurrence might be associated with increased exposure of the cows to mastitis pathogens in their environment due to high stocking density, dirty ground, infected utensils, poor ventilation, and high humidity. There is also a statistically significant difference in cows with a previous history of mastitis compared to cows with no previous history of mastitis. A similar finding was reported by Mekonnen et al. (Mekonnen et al. 2017) in Ethiopia and Kumar et al. (Kumar et al. 2016) in India. However, it contrasted with findings by Abebe et al. (Abebe et al. 2016), Ethiopia, who did not find any association between a history of mastitis and future occurrence of mastitis.

It was found that milking cows with lesion and/or tick infestation on the udder and/or teat skin had a higher prevalence rate (58.1%) compared to animals without this factor (16.8%). There was also a statistically significant association between the lesion and/or tick infestation (OR: 0.15; 95% CI: 0.07, 0.28; p = 0.000) and the occurrence of mastitis. A similar observation has been recorded in previous studies (Getahun et al. 2008, Belay et al. 2022). This is because changes to teat tissue, particularly the skin of the barrel, teat‐end, and teat canal may favour penetration of bacteria into the udder and increase the risk of new mastitis infection (Hamann et al. 1994).

In the present study, the isolation rate of S. aureus was 2.6% and nearly similar to the 5.5% reported by Dabele et al. (Dabele et al. 2021). On the contrary, a higher isolation rate for S. aureus (24.2%) by Zeryehun and Abera (Zeryehun and Abera 2017) and 51.2% by Abebe et al. (Abebe et al. 2016) had reported in selected districts of Eastern Harrarghe Zone and dairy herds at Hawassa milk shed areas respectively. The variation in the isolation rate of S. aureus reported from mastitic milk samples from different districts of the country might be due to differences in sample size, the agro ecological zone, and management practices of the milking cows at different places. Furthermore, in our study, we used Biolog Microbial ID System for the confirmation of S. aureus isolates which delivers a high level of accuracy that is comparable to molecular methods. However, most of the previous studies (Khasapane et al. 2024, Grima et al. 2021) were based on phenotypic identification, which may misidentify and underestimate the true prevalence of S. aureus involved mastitis.

The present study is not without limitations. For instance, this study identified only five isolates of S. aureus and evaluated their resistance against ten different antibiotics. The small sample size in this study limits its statistical power to make broader conclusion. Consequently, the antimicrobial susceptibility test results should be considered a preliminary finding with limited insights.

All isolates subjected to an antimicrobial susceptibility test revealed that 100% susceptible to amoxicillin‐clavulanate, cefoxitin, ciprofloxacin, chloramphenicol, oxacillin, trimethoprim‐sulphamethoxazole, and gentamycin as it has been shown in Table 3. On the contrary, zero susceptibility of S. aureus to penicillin G was recorded (Table 3). This might be happened due extensive misuse or overuse, possibly due to unregulated access and lack of veterinary oversight. This is similar to the findings that reported 0% susceptibility of S. aureus to pencillin G by Marama et al. (Marama et al. 2016) and Asmelash et al. (Asmelash et al. 2016) in Holota area and around Kombolcha respectively. Apart from, these findings, Abera et al. (Abera et al. 2013) around Adama have also recorded 5.6% susceptibility to this drug. Besides this, the present study has also demonstrated similarly zero susceptibility of S. aureus to tetracycline which is contrary to the reports of 7.5% around Kombolcha (Asmelash et al. 2016). The regular and indiscriminate use of these regularly used antibiotics for the treatment of cows may be the cause of S. aureus developing resistance, which may lead to the spread of resistant strains in the research area. Moreover, the presence of resistance for tetracycline, and penicillin G was that these drugs have been used commonly in the country and in the study area for so many years both in the veterinary and public health sectors. Our finding was consistent with research output from past studies conducted in other nations, which suggested that extended and indiscriminate use of particular antibiotics could lead to the development of resistance (Calderón‐Jaimes et al. 2002). Since antibiotics for food‐producing animals can be purchased without prescription in Ethiopia (Beyene et al. 2017), likely, that the excessive or improper use of tetracycline and penicillin may also have contributed to the selection pressure of resistant clones and the subsequent high resistance levels found in this study.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The results of the present study indicated that bovine mastitis (44.2%) is highly prevalent and a major health problem among dairy cattle in pastoral areas of Uba Debretsehaye district, Southern Ethiopia. The potential risk factors identified for the occurrence of mastitis were tick infestation on udder and/ or injury, in addition to previous history of mastitis. The S. aureus isolates was also tested to some commonly used antibiotic agents demonstrated that the isolates have very low susceptibility, 0% to penicillin G and tetracycline, but, good susceptibility was seen to the rest antibiotics including oxacillin and trimethoprim‐sulphamethoxazole. The result of this study underscores the critical need for antimicrobial stewardship programs and warrants effective control measures to mitigate the impact of mastitis on dairy production and animal health in the study areas. Thus, appropriate treatment of cows during the dry and lactation periods should be practiced; management, housing, and environmental sanitation should be improved. Further detailed epidemiological, microbiological, and economic impact analysis studies are suggested at in rural Ethiopia settings to enhance the existing control and prevention strategies.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: Fitsum Dulo: conceptualization, supervision, writing–original draft, formal analysis, methodology, and software. Mabure Shille: conceptualization, data collection, formal analysis, methodology and editing. Shubisa Abera: conceptualization, formal analysis, methodology, and software. Seblewongel Ayechew: conceptualization, resources, formal analysis, methodology, resources and editing.

sec.title: Ethics Statement
sec.p: The study protocol was reviewed and approved by the institutional animal care and use for scientific research committee of Wolaita Sodo University (Ref. No: WSU‐IRRC/018/2022). The farmers enrolled for this study were those who gave their consent orally after the purpose and methods of study were explained to them.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

sec.title: Peer Review
sec.p: The peer review history for this article is available at https://publons.com/publon/10.1002/vms3.70503.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors would like to acknowledge Sebata Animal Health Institute for material and technical support during the study period. Moreover, we would like to appreciate and thank animal owners and animal health assistants of the Uba Debretsehaye districts for their cooperation during sample collection.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The data used to support the findings of the study are available on reasonable request from the corresponding author.

ref.mixed-citation: Halasa, T., K.Huijps, O.Østerås, and H.Hogeveen. 2007. “Economic Effects of Bovine Mastitis and Mastitis Management: A Review.” Veterinary Quarterly29, no. 1: 18–31.1747178810.1080/01652176.2007.9695224
